subject: English
overall_learning_objectives: Analyze the nuances of verbal and non-verbal communication in social settings
Identify the difference between assertive, passive, and aggressive speech
Understand the impact of tone, pitch, and body language on the message delivered
Practice conflict resolution through 'I-statements' and active listening
Develop a vocabulary for expressing complex emotions and constructive feedback
Evaluate social scenarios to determine the most effective communicative response

lesson_plan.title: The Art of Social Grace
topics_covered: Defining 'The Right Thing' as contextual and cultural
The components of a message: Words vs. Tone vs. Body Language
teaching_strategy: Interactive lecture using the '7-38-55 Rule' (Words, Tone, Body Language)
Video clips or live demonstrations of the same sentence said in different tones
student_activities: Group activity: Try to convey an emotion (e.g., anger, joy) using only a neutral sentence like 'The weather is nice today'
Fill out a self-assessment on their own communication style

lesson_plan.title: Tact and Diplomacy
teaching_strategy: Guided reading of the chapter's section on difficult conversations
Teaching the 'Sandwich Method' for giving feedback (Compliment-Critique-Compliment)
Case study analysis of a social 'faux pas'
student_activities: Rewrite 'blunt' sentences to make them 'tactful' (e.g., 'Your work is bad' to 'I think this could be improved by...')
Vocabulary matching exercise with synonyms and antonyms

lesson_plan.title: The Power of 'I-Statements'
teaching_strategy: Role-reversal demonstration: Showing how 'I-statements' de-escalate tension
Discussion on taking ownership of one’s feelings
student_activities: Role-play a common school conflict (e.g., a friend being late) using the formula

lesson_plan.title: Active Listening and Paraphrasing
topics_covered: Listening as a cognitive process, not just a physical one
The skill of paraphrasing to ensure understanding
teaching_strategy: Activity: 'Back-to-Back' drawing (One student describes an image, the other draws it without looking)
Teaching 'Clarification Questions' (e.g., 'What I hear you saying is... is that right?')
student_activities: Engage in a 'Paraphrasing Circle' where each student summarizes the previous speaker's point before adding their own

lesson_plan.title: Digital and Formal Etiquette
topics_covered: Netiquette: Saying the right thing in emails and texts
Formal speech for special occasions (introductions, thank-you speeches)
teaching_strategy: Comparing a 'rude' text message with a 'professional' one
Discussion on emojis and how they replace tone in writing
Introduction to the 'Introduction Speech' (Introducing a guest/friend)
student_activities: Draft a formal email to a teacher requesting a deadline extension
Practice introducing a classmate to the 'Principal' (role-play)

lesson_plan.title: Synthesis and Mastery Quiz
topics_covered: Cumulative review of all communication tools
Application of tools to complex scenarios
teaching_strategy: The 'Communication Scavenger Hunt': Finding correct phrases for various scenarios hidden in the room
Interactive Quiz: Identifying the communication style (Assertive/Passive/Aggressive) of different quotes

teaching_aids: Audio/Video clips of different speaking tones
Scenario cards for the Scavenger Hunt
teacher_tip: Focus heavily on 'The Pause.' Teach students that saying the right thing often requires a 3-second pause to process emotions before responding. Encourage them to be 'social detectives' and observe communication in the hallway or cafeteria.
